g.data-narration: Welcome! This lecture covers Python modules essential for building maintainable IoT systems.
g.text: Building Maintainable IoT Systems with Reusable Python Code

g.data-narration: A module is simply a .py file containing Python code that can be reused across multiple IoT projects.
Modules are crucial in IoT for organizing complex systems into logical components like sensors, communication, and data processing.
Creating a module is simple: save your Python code in a .py file with functions, classes, and module-level variables.

text.tspan: Save as .py file → Import anywhere → Reuse across projects

g.data-narration: Learn three import methods: basic import, selective import with from, and alias import using as keyword.
Python searches modules in order: current directory, PYTHONPATH, standard library, then site-packages.
Every module has special attributes: __name__ for module name, __file__ for file path, and __doc__ for documentation.
Key standard library modules for IoT: json for serialization, time/datetime for timestamps, os for file operations, sys for system control.
The __name__ variable equals __main__ when run directly, allowing test code inside the guard to run only during direct execution.
g.text: # Test code runs only when file executed directly
# Does NOT run when imported as module

g.data-narration: IoT projects use packages - directories with __init__.py - containing related modules for sensors, communication, and utilities.
This example shows a sensor module with functions to read environmental data and format it as JSON for IoT transmission.
A config module centralizes IoT settings like broker address, port, and sensor intervals for easy modification.
This diagram shows typical module dependencies in an IoT system - main program imports sensors and communication modules.
Key exam points: remember import syntax variations, module search order, __name__ usage, and common module errors like circular imports.
g.text: import vs from...import vs import as

g.data-narration: In summary: modules are reusable .py files, use import statements to access them, and organize IoT code into logical components for maintainability.

text.tspan: .py file with reusable code (functions, classes, variables)
import, from...import, as for accessing modules
"__main__" when run directly, prevents test code on import
